story:
When Vada King was twelve, she and her mother wrapped their Toyota Supra around a lamppost. They were in the middle of a screaming match—so loud, so hot, that later Vada couldn’t remember what started it. Her mother threw her hands up in anger, and Vada, burning with a fury she couldn’t hold, shoved her mother’s right shoulder hard toward the passenger side. Her mother’s feet slid, searching for the floor, found the gas instead. The Supra leapt forward. Metal shrieked. The right side slammed a lamppost and folded around it.

Her mother’s body bent at wrong angles; pain drove her into blackness. Vada’s head shattered the passenger window; blood dripped onto the pavement. Teenagers from an ice cream shop called for help. In the ambulance, it seemed like they might both make it—until her mother’s heart stopped. Too much pain, too much fear. Gone.

Through her teens, Vada carried staple scars in her scalp, an elbow that didn’t work right, and a hollow wish that she’d gone with her mother. She learned people could be cruel. A classmate hissed murderer in her face. Sometimes Vada resented the moment her mother took her hands off the wheel. Sometimes she hated herself for shoving. She told a therapist she wanted to erase it all and pretend her mother died a normal way. He asked, Is there any death that isn’t traumatic? She never stopped turning that over.

When she went to college, it was a different level of escape. No one from high school went with her. There was no one that knew of the sad, sad way Vada King lost her mother. No one pitied her with tilted heads and pouting lips. Unless she told them about it. Which she didn’t. There was constantly something occupying her mind. She didn’t have time to think about her mother’s laugh, or how their twisted Supra looked in pictures. She was an excellent student and fairly social as well. She had a goal in life, and was determined to achieve it. Despite this determination, her senior year was rough. Doubt regarding her plans in life began to creep in. She started to not know what she wanted to do with her future. The space in her life where her mother fit in felt emptier than ever. 

That’s when she met Toby: soccer player, biology major, stubborn cowlick, a kindness that didn’t feel like pity. He chased parties until he learned she hated them, then he made picnics and slow walks instead. When he asked about her parents, warning bells blared inside her, but she chose him. On a bench for two hours, she told him the good, the bad, and finally the end. At one point she wiped her face and told him, with calm certainty, that they would marry in a year and a half, live twenty-five minutes from her mother’s cemetery. They cried and laughed together. That night she told him she loved him.

She graduated with a psychology degree. They married a year and a half later. Vada wouldn’t leave the state where her mother lay. Toby understood and bought a house twenty-five minutes from the cemetery. For three years they were young and happy and working—steady, ordinary joy. Then the pregnancy test showed two pink lines. Toby went pale, then grinned like a boy who’d just discovered what he wanted. By the time Vada held their June-born daughter, her tears were both full and empty—new life in her arms.

Their daughter grew sunny and curious, repelled by carrots, adored by dogs. Money got tight. A job across the country wasn’t a choice; it was rescue. On moving day, boxes stacked and floors bare, Vada grabbed Toby’s sleeve and whispered. He said yes. They drove to the cemetery.

Under the willow, Vada’s heart clenched. She knelt with her daughter and nodded toward the stone. Read it to me. The small voice sounded out: Holly Lauren King. Beloved mother, daughter, and granddaughter. She turned, beaming. Your mommy’s name was Holly? And my name is Holly Lauren! Laughter broke something open. Healing slipped through.

They left for the long drive west. As the SUV merged onto the highway, Vada felt that old acceleration. Only this time, holding fast to the people she loved, she wasn’t afraid.
